coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly carbon dots induced thermally stable vertical alignment in planar anchored nematic liquid crystal
coredata.dc:creator: Priscilla, P
Varshney, Deepanshu
Prakash, Jai
Kumar, Sandeep
Singh, Ashwani
Malik, Praveen
Supreet,
Gathania, Arvind K.
Castagna, Riccardo
Lucchetta, Daniele Eugenio
Singh, Gautam
coredata.dc:description: We report the induction of thermally stable vertical alignment of nematic liquid crystal, 4-cyano-4′-pentylbiphenyl (5CB), in a planar anchored cell by doping with eco-friendly carbon dots (CDs, diameter ∼7–8 nm), investigated through thermal, optical, electro-optical and dielectric spectroscopic techniques. Cross-polarized optical textures and voltage-dependent optical transmission measurements demonstrate the induction of vertical alignment of CDs-5CB composites with concentration of CDs (≥0.3 wt%) in planar anchored sample cells at room temperature. Moreover, the frequency-bias-concentration dependent dielectric studies done also confirms the induced vertical alignment. For instance, the relative dielectric permittivity measured on CDs-5CB composites (CDs ≥ 0.3 wt%) in planar anchored sample cells at bias voltage of 0 V and 30 V DC are almost equal and the presence of a short axis molecular relaxation at 0 V confirms the induced vertical alignment. The concentration and bias dependence of short axis molecular relaxation frequency are also discussed. It appears that CDs are able to align 5CB molecules vertically at the substrate by overcoming the pre-existing planar anchoring. Such induced alignment would propagate through the bulk of sample cell because of the coherence length of nematic liquid crystal molecules and eventually the whole sample obtains vertical alignment. We anticipate the great utility of these composites in the production of eco-friendly liquid crystal displays and other photonic devices, sensors, etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nematic liquid crystal
Carbon dots
Composites
Alignment

Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors Ms. Priscilla P and Dr. Gautam Singh thank to Amity University Uttar Pradesh, Noida, India for its continuous support and encouragement in their research work. Dr. Jai Prakash and Dr. Gautam Singh are grateful to the Council of Science and Technology (CST), U. P., India for financial assistance under CST funded research project (Project ID: 1960). Dr. Supreet would like to acknowledge the support provided under the DST-FIST Grant No.SR/FST/PS-I/2019/68 of Govt. of India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical textures of Nematic phase in (a) 0.00 wt%, (b) 0.03 wt%, (c) 0.05 wt%, (d) 0.1 wt%, (e) 0.25 wt%, (f) 0.3 wt%, (g) 0.5 wt% and (h) 1.0 wt% CDs-5CB composites. The scale bar is 100 μm. P, A and R denote the polarizer, analyzer, and rubbing direction in the sample cell, respectively. The temperature of pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites was kept at 26 °C.
The voltage dependent optical transmission of pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 30 °C. The ac square pulse of 1KHz frequency of varying amplitude (0–20 V) was applied for the V-T measurements.

The schematic diagram of interaction between CDs and 5CB molecules in CDs-5CB composites infiltrated in planar anchored sample cell: (a) zero, (b) low, (c) medium and (d) higher concentration of CDs. Herein, red ellipsoid and green sphere denote the 5CB molecule and CD, respectively. R denotes the rubbing direction in the sample cell. The size of CD is about three times larger in diameter than the length of 5CB molecule.

The frequency dependent relative dielectric permittivity (ε) of pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 26 °C temperature at (a) 0 V and (b) 30 V DC bias field, respectively. The thickness of planar anchored sample cells used was 5.0 μm.

The concentration dependent relative dielectric permittivity (ε) of CDs-5CB composites at 0 V and 30 V at 10 KHz. The 0 V refers no change in the alignment whereas 30 V refers to the change in the alignment from planar to vertical or improve in the vertical alignment induced by CDs.
The frequency dependent dielectric loss of pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 26 °C: (a) 0 V and (b) 30 V, respectively.
The comparison of short axis molecular relaxation frequency in 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 0 V and 30 V.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly carbon dots induced thermally stable vertical alignment in planar anchored nematic liquid crystal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the induction of thermally stable vertical alignment of nematic liquid crystal, 4-cyano-4′-pentylbiphenyl (5CB), in a planar anchored cell by doping with eco-friendly carbon dots (CDs, diameter ∼7–8 nm), investigated through thermal, optical, electro-optical and dielectric spectroscopic techniques. Cross-polarized optical textures and voltage-dependent optical transmission measurements demonstrate the induction of vertical alignment of CDs-5CB composites with concentration of CDs (≥0.3 wt%) in planar anchored sample cells at room temperature. Moreover, the frequency-bias-concentration dependent dielectric studies done also confirms the induced vertical alignment. For instance, the relative dielectric permittivity measured on CDs-5CB composites (CDs ≥ 0.3 wt%) in planar anchored sample cells at bias voltage of 0 V and 30 V DC are almost equal and the presence of a short axis molecular relaxation at 0 V confirms the induced vertical alignment. The concentration and bias dependence of short axis molecular relaxation frequency are also discussed. It appears that CDs are able to align 5CB molecules vertically at the substrate by overcoming the pre-existing planar anchoring. Such induced alignment would propagate through the bulk of sample cell because of the coherence length of nematic liquid crystal molecules and eventually the whole sample obtains vertical alignment. We anticipate the great utility of these composites in the production of eco-friendly liquid crystal displays and other photonic devices, sensors, etc.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the burgeoning field of liquid crystals (LCs), nematic liquid crystals (NLCs), a long-range orientationally ordered and positionally disordered fluid have remained the most exploited LC phase for the fundamental and device applications. This is primarily due to its simple phase structure and ease of alignment over large areas. In NLC phase, the molecules, on an average, are oriented along a common direction, defined as the director (n) which is highly responsive to the external stimuli (temperature, electric/magnetic field, optical field, etc.). Most importantly, the electric field dependent director reorientation of NLCs forms the basis of their numerous applications in science, industry, and medical [1–4]. For instance, they are widely used in display devices like TV, laptop, mobile screens and in non-display devices such as sensors, lasers, lenses, gratings, filters, magnetic resonance, microscopic diagnosis of cancer, etc [5–9]. However, their relatively slower switching speed, higher threshold voltage, lower optical contrast, limited viewing angle, etc. are the limiting factors in applications of NLCs. Consequently, researchers around the globe are working on to explore new ways to improve upon these factors by modifying the properties of NLCs. Under this framework, various methods to enhance the properties of NLCs have been sought after and one such method which has become a point of great discussion and exploration is the dispersion of nanoparticles (NPs) in NLC matrix. Recently, much work has been reported, and the dispersion of NPs has produced significant changes in the properties of host NLC materials [10–18]. Among all NPs, the quantum dots (QDs, NPs of size ∼2–10 nm) are being investigated the most due to their exceptional optical and electronic properties. It has been observed that dispersion of QDs tends to produce significant impact on the properties of NLCs and further enabled their use in various fields of application. For instance, the dispersion of QDs in NLCs led to the (i) change in the molecular alignment, (ii) tunable pretilt angle, (iii) reduction in threshold/saturation voltage, (iv) enhancement in photoluminescence and (v) shift in clearing (nematic-isotropic transition) temperature [19–24].

Various QDs are being investigated however, in recent years; researchers have pointed out that the QDs should be eco-friendly (i.e., without or minimal toxicity) [25]. Among the eco-friendly QDs, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or Carbon dots (CDs) have obtained high favor due to their low cost, eco-friendliness, bio-compatibility, higher photoluminescence with large quantum yield, higher photostability, etc. They are frequently used in applications such as solar cells, carbon-based LEDs for TV screens, super capacitors, sensors, environmental pollution control, information encryption and majorly in biomedical applications [26–31]. Besides these applications, interestingly, CDs are also recently used as dopants in LCs to bring changes in the properties of the host material. For instance, Urbanski et al.
[32] reported that the doping of chemically and thermally stable CDs (diameter: 2.5 ± 0.5 nm) at concentrations up to 2.5 wt% in the NLC material (LC1, Phenylpyrimidine based NLC) resulted into its lower value of apparent threshold voltage and elastic constants but higher values of the rotational viscosity. However, at 2.5 wt% and higher concentration of CDs, an increase in the pre-tilt angle of ∼8° in planar aligned cell at lower temperatures was found and attributed to the residing of CDs at the confining interfaces. Eskalen et al. [33] demonstrated (i) the increase in threshold voltage and splay elastic constant and (ii) decrease in clearing temperature of NLC (E7), with increase in the dopant concentration of water-soluble fluorescent CDs (diameter: 2.37 ± 0.42 nm), using the dielectric and thermal studies. Praseetha et al.
[34] reported the significant increase in the (i) third order nonlinear optical absorption coefficient and (ii) third order susceptibility of NLC (5PCH) by doping organophilic CDs (diameter: ∼8–10 nm), and predicted the potential application in nonlinear photonic devices and optical limiters. Pandey et al. [35] depicted the quenching of photoluminescence and increase in the zeta potential in CDs (diameter: ∼1–3 nm) doped NLC (7CB) composites as compared to the pristine NLC sample. Rastogi et al.
[36] demonstrated the improvement in the molecular alignment, conductivity, dielectric strength, and relaxation frequency of carbonaceous oil palm leaf QDs doped NLC composites as compared to the pristine NLC (E48, eutectic mixture). The stability of the suspensions (i.e., composites) was confirmed through zeta potential measurements. The decrease in the specific and total power loss, a decrement in the activation energy of the composites was also monitored which eventually made them suitable for low power consumption display devices. Moreover, Gao et al.
[37] investigated the dynamic holographic display properties of CDs (diameter ∼5 nm) doped NLC thin film, useful for real time holographic three-dimensional display screen. Interestingly, the recorded diffraction efficiency of doped thin film was 30% even at a low applied electric field of 0.2 V/μm with low readout light power of 8mW/mm2. The build-up and self-erasure time of hologram in the doped thin film was found to be fast enough to exploit the video refresh rate. The authors predicted the use of these CDs doped NLC thin films in future true three-dimensional TV display. It is worth pointing out here that Sharma et al. [38] demonstrated the uniform vertical alignment of NLCs by using the inkjet printing of CDs on the substrates of the ITO sample cells. Interestingly, this method was also found to be equally useful for other nanoparticles (ex. Gold) to induce vertical alignment to NLCs [39]. Moreover, CDs were also being used as dopant to tune the dielectric and electro-optical properties of ferroelectric liquid crystals [40].

It is worth pointing out here that the work done so far on CDs doped NLC composites mainly revolve around the impact of CDs on the clearing temperature, alignment, optical (linear/non-linear), electro-optical and dielectric properties [32–37]. Some interesting results have been obtained regarding alignment (i) Urbanski et al. [32] reported the observation of mixture of partial planar (initially perfectly planar aligned) and induced vertical alignment of NLC by CDs (concentration ≥2.5 wt%) at lower temperature of NLC but revoked the planar alignment at higher temperaturenearer the N-I transition and (ii) Rastogi et al. [36] demonstrated the improvement in the pre-existing planar alignment of NLC by the dopant CDs. However, there are no reports of induction of high quality thermally stable vertical alignment in NLCs by dopant CDs. Herein, we report the induction of thermally stable vertical alignment in the planar anchored NLC (5CB) by doping of organo soluble CDs. The confirmation of the induced vertical alignment was demonstrated using cross-polarised optical microscopy and frequency dependent dielectric spectroscopy on samples with variation of concentration of CDs and different temperatures. Our results can be attributed to the overcoming of planar anchoring of NLC molecules by CDs and forcing them to change their orientation from planar to vertical.

The NLC material 5CB (4-cyano-4′-pentylbiphenyl) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without any further purification. The phase sequence of 5CB is Cr 25N 35 Iso. The organosoluble carbon dots (CDs) with uniform size (diameter ∼7–8 nm) synthesized and characterized by Mahesh and co-workers was procured and utilized as dopant [41]. The planar anchored ITO sample cells (anti-parallel rubbed polyimide) of thickness 5 µm were purchased from Instec, Inc. USA and used for optical texture and dielectric studies of pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites. Firstly, we dissolved CDs in chloroform (0.1 w/v%) and prepared a solution of CDs dispersed in chloroform. Afterwards, 5 mg of 5CB was weighed in the seven separate glass vials and then added the selected volume of CDs’ dispersed solution to prepare the CDs-5CB composites with CDs’ concentration (wt%) of 0.03 wt%, 0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.3 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 1.0 wt%, respectively. These composites were then ultrasonicated for 2 h to get a complete uniform dispersion of the CDs in the host NLC (5CB). Thereafter, they were kept in oven at 65 °C for one hour to evaporate the solvent chloroform from the composites. Finally, the pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites were infiltrated into planar anchored ITO sample cells in the isotropic phase through capillary action and then cooled down to the room temperature.
(b)
Characterization methods

To check the quality of alignment of CDs-5CB composites as compared to pure 5CB, the optical textures were recorded using cross-polarized optical microscope (Nikon Eclipse LV100POL, Japan) equipped with a CCD Q imaging camera (model: Q28378) linked with Linksys software and the temperature of the cell was maintained using temperature controller (T96, Linkam, UK) connected to a heating/freezing stage (THMS600E, Linkam, UK) with an accuracy of ±0.01 °C. To determine the electro-optical parameters, LC cells were subjected to a square wave pulse (1 kHz) from a function generator (Tektronix AFG3021B) with variable amplitude. The monochromatic light from Helium-Neon laser (Melles Griot, 5mW, 632.8 nm) was transmitted through the LC cells which were positioned between crossed polarizers. The transmitted light was then channelled into a photodiode (Instec, PD02) linked with digital multimeter (Keysight 34465A 6 ½ digits). The differential scanning calorimeter (STA 8000, Perkin Elmer) was used for the temperature range of 25 °C–40 °C (heating and cooling cycles, scanning rate 5 °C/minute) to detect the changes in the clearing temperature (i.e., Isotropic to nematic phase) of the composites as compared to pure 5CB. Moreover, the temperature-bias-concentration dependent dielectric studies were carried out using a LCR meter (E4980A, Keysight, U.S.A.) in the frequency range of 20 Hz to 2 MHz. A temperature controller coupled with hot air oven was used to regulate the temperature. For the dielectric measurements, the applied AC voltage was kept 500 mV and DC bias voltage (0–30 V) was applied.

In the research of liquid crystals and their composites with nanoparticles, the cross-polarized optical microscope is primarily used to record the optical textures and to examine the type and quality of the alignment. The optical textures of pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites in planar anchored ITO sample cells at 26 °C are shown in the Fig. 1 . As seen in Fig. 1(b) & (c), the quality of planar alignment of 0.03 and 0.05 wt% CDs-5CB composites is more or less same to the pure 5CB (Fig. 1a), as expected due to very feeble dopant concentration. The optical texture of 0.1 wt% CDs-5CB composite appears to be planar with diminished optical transmission (Fig. 1d) which is quite different than pure 5CB and 0.03 & 0.05 wt% CDs-5CB composites. Interestingly, the optical transmission decreases and textures indicative of defects occur in 0.25 wt% CDs-5CB composite and which indicates the changes in the orientation of the NLC (Fig. 1e). However, when the concentration of CDs in 5CB is higher than 0.25 wt%, the optical texture becomes dark and which eventually signifies that the alignment of the composites is completely changed to vertical (Fig. 1f-h). To confirm more about the induced vertical alignment, the voltage dependent optical transmission (V-T) measurements [42] were done on pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites filled in planar anchored sample cells. The results are shown in the Fig. 2 . The observed behaviour of V-T curve for pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites (0.03, 0.05 and 0.1 wt%) are qualitatively similar in nature except for the threshold voltage (Vth), saturation voltage (Vsat) and transmission intensity. For instance, Vth firstly increases for the 0.03 wt% composite and then decreases for higher concentration as compared to pure 5CB. Similar behaviour is also observed for Vsat. But in the case of 0.25 wt% composite, both Vth and Vsat are reduced by ∼50% to that of pure 5CB. This could be attributed to the pre-existing partial vertical alignment of 5CB molecules in the 0.25 wt% composite (Fig. 1e) and eventually hence lesser required to obtain the complete vertical alignment. The concentration dependent Vth and Vsat of CDs-5CB composites is shown in Figure S3. However, in the composites (concentration greater than 0.25 wt%), V-T curves demonstrate that the optical transmission remains almost zero throughout the applied voltage range and which correlates well with optical textures (Fig. 1f-h). Such behaviour of voltage dependent optical transmission again confirms the induced vertical alignment of 5CB in the composites with concentration more than 0.25 wt% CDs. In other words, both optical textures and V-T measurements clearly demonstrate that the dopant CDs can induce the vertical alignment of 5CB infiltrated into a planar anchored ITO sample cell.

To understand the mechanism of induced vertical alignment of 5CB molecules by dopant CDs, we have proposed the adhoc schematic model as shown in the Fig. 3 . At low concentration, CDs are less in number and move in the bulk of the sample and may slightly change the orientation of planar anchored 5CB molecules (Fig. 3b). By increasing the concentration of CDs (i.e., medium concentration), most of the CDs would settle at the substrate and some of them still remains in the bulk of sample. Surprisingly, after settling at the substrate, CDs try to overcome the planar anchoring and eventually align 5CB molecules vertically the at the substrate (Fig. 3c). By further increasing the concentration of CDs (i.e., higher concentration) in 5CB, CDs would almost overcome the planar anchoring at the substrate and all 5CB molecules at the substrate would align vertically (Fig. 3d). Due to self-assembly nature of nematic liquid crystal (i.e., 5CB), the induced vertical alignment at the substrate would propagate throughout the bulk of the sample cell and entire sample cell would have the vertical alignment.

The thermal stability of the alignment of the liquid crystal plays a very important role in the performance of display and non-display devices [43,44]. After the confirmation of induced vertical alignment of 5CB by dopant CDs at 26 °C, the stability of alignment was checked throughout the nematic phase. Interestingly, the induced vertical alignment remained stable throughout the nematic phase of the composites (Figure S1). Moreover, to check the impact of CDs on the clearing temperature of 5CB, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was done in both heating and cooling cycles at 5 °C per minute (Figure S2). The maximum decrement in clearing temperature of the composites was ∼1.1 °C and ∼1.5 °C in heating and cooling, respectively as compared to a pure 5CB sample (Table 1, ESI). This further confirms that the doping of CDs up to 1 wt% does not severely affect the nematic phase width of 5CB.

To understand more about the effect of dopant CDs on the alignment of 5CB, we used the frequency dependent dielectric spectroscopy as it probes the molecular dynamics [45,46]. Fig. 4 shows the frequency dependence of the relative dielectric permittivity (ε) for pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 26 °C at 0 V and 30 V DC bias. As seen in Fig. 4(a), ε at 0 V increases with increase in concentration of CDs except for 0.03 wt% CDs-5CB composite. For higher concentrations such as 0.3, 0.5 and 1 wt% the values of ε at 0 V (Fig. 4a) are almost similar to the values of ε at 30 V (i.e., parallel component of ε, Fig. 4b). This again confirms that the orientation of NLC director in composites at 0 V is changed from planar to vertical due to the CDs and which further corroborates well with optical textures (Fig. 1f-h). This could be attributed to the capability of CDs to overcome the strong planar anchoring of the sample cell at the substrates and orient 5CB molecules upright as explained earlier.

The concentration dependent relative dielectric permittivity (ε) at 10 kHz of CDs-5CB composites at 0 V and 30 V is shown in the Fig. 5 . As seen in the figure, the value of ε at 0 V and 30 V of CDs-5CB composites are almost equal (i.e., overlap) at concentration ≥0.3 wt%. This again confirms that at CDs’ concentration ≥0.3 wt%, the alignment of 5CB in the planar anchored nematic sample cell changes to vertical.

The presence of short axis molecular relaxation in the 5CB and CDs-5CB composites also confirms the presence of vertical alignment [44,45]. The short axis molecular relaxation at 0 V is observed in CDs-5CB composites with concentration ≥0.3 wt% [Fig. 6 (a)] and which confirms the vertical alignment of 5CB. However, at 30 V, the short axis molecular relaxation is observed in pure 5CB and all CDs-5CB composites [Fig. 6(b)] and confirms the vertical alignment in all samples as expected.

Fig. 7 shows the comparison of short axis molecular relaxation frequency in 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 0 V and 30 V DC bias. The frequency related to short axis molecular relaxation is not observed in 5CB and CDs-5CB composites with concentration <0.3 wt% at 0 V bias and attributed to the planar alignment of pure 5CB and these low concentration composites. However, at 30 V DC bias, the relaxation frequency is observed in pure 5CB and CDs-5CB composites as expected due to change of planar to vertical alignment of 5CB molecules by external DC bias voltage. At concentration of CDs ≥ 0.3 wt%, the short axis relaxation frequency is almost the same for 0 V and 30 V DC bias and which confirms that vertical alignment of 5CB induced by CDs and DC bias are of almost the same nature.

The effect of doping of eco-friendly CDs on the molecular alignment of planar anchored 5CB sample and clearing temperature is thoroughly investigated using cross-polarized microscopy, voltage dependent optical transmission, dielectric spectroscopy, and differential scanning calorimeter. The cross-polarized optical textures and optical transmission measurements on 5CB and CDs-5CB composites clearly demonstrate the change in the orientation of 5CB molecules from planar to vertical (i.e., bright to dark state) with increase in the concentration of dopant CDs. The complete vertical alignment is achieved for CDs’ concentration (≥0.3 wt%). Interestingly, such induced vertical alignment is thermally stable throughout the nematic phase, monitored through the temperature dependent optical textures. The frequency-bias-concentration dependent dielectric measurements also demonstrate (i) the almost identical value of relative dielectric permittivity of CDs-5CB composites (≥0.3 wt%) at 0 V and 30 V and (ii) the presence of short axis molecular relaxation at 0 V, again confirms the induction of vertical alignment in planar anchored sample cell. The effect of CDs’ concentration and bias on the short axis molecular relaxation frequency is also observed. Moreover, thermal studies show that the nematic phase width is not severely affected by the doping of CDs up to 1.0 wt%. The induced alignment may be attributed to the overcoming of planar anchoring of 5CB molecules at the substrate surface by dopant CDs and eventually align them vertically. Afterwards, such induced alignment would propagate throughout the bulk of the sample cell due to self-assembly nature of liquid crystal molecules and the whole sample cell eventually become vertically aligned. Our results clearly indicate that these CDs-5CB composites would be useful in the manufacturing of the next generation eco-friendly LCDs. Moreover, we propose that these CDs can also be incorporated in the synthesis of polymer composites to understand the effect they bring in the devices such as laser [47–49], holographic gratings [50], photonic devices and biosensors [51].

Priscilla P: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Validation, Visualization. Deepanshu Varshney: Data curation. Jai Prakash: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Sandeep Kumar: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Ashwani Singh: Data curation. Praveen Malik: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Supreet: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Arvind K. Gathania: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Riccardo Castagna: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Daniele Eugenio Lucchetta: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Gautam Singh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors Ms. Priscilla P and Dr. Gautam Singh thank to Amity University Uttar Pradesh, Noida, India for its continuous support and encouragement in their research work. Dr. Jai Prakash and Dr. Gautam Singh are grateful to the Council of Science and Technology (CST), U. P., India for financial assistance under CST funded research project (Project ID: 1960). Dr. Supreet would like to acknowledge the support provided under the DST-FIST Grant No.SR/FST/PS-I/2019/68 of Govt. of India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.122318.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmentally friendly quantum-dot color filters for ultra-high-definition liquid crystal displays
